name: Palantir
overview: Palantir is a US software company that builds data and AI platforms for defence and government — its core products are Gotham (intelligence/military), Foundry (data integration) and AIP, which plugs large language models into secure, classified environments. Its public defence footprint is dominated by the US Department of Defense's Project Maven AI program and a decade-long, up-to-$10bn Army software agreement, plus next-generation Army targeting hardware (TITAN). It is increasingly the same vendor across the US, NATO and the UK, which is what draws both large contracts and vendor-lock-in scrutiny.

established.label: The intelligence/military platform
established.plain: Palantir's original software for defence and intelligence agencies. It pulls together scattered data — satellite imagery, location feeds, reports — and lets analysts search it, map it and spot patterns, so military and intelligence staff can make decisions faster. It is the product most associated with Palantir's national-security work.

established.label: LLMs inside classified systems
established.plain: AIP is Palantir's layer for putting large language models (the technology behind chatbots) to work on sensitive, secret data — with auditing, data masking and a mandatory human sign-off before any high-stakes action. In plain terms, it lets an organisation use modern AI on its own classified information without the AI being able to act on its own.

established.label: AI that fuses the battlefield picture
established.plain: Built for the Pentagon's flagship AI effort, Project Maven, this system ingests data from many sources — satellites, sensors, location data — and uses AI to fuse it onto a single map and flag potential targets, helping commanders see the battlefield and decide faster. The Defense Department signed an initial $480m, five-year contract in May 2024 covering several combatant commands and the Joint Staff.

established.label: Next-gen targeting ground station
established.plain: TITAN (Tactical Intelligence Targeting Access Node) is a physical ground system — built into trucks and tactical vehicles — that links sensors in space and the field to the weapons that act on them, speeding up long-range targeting. The Army awarded Palantir $178m in March 2024 to build 10 systems; Palantir delivered the first two on schedule in March 2025.

established.label: Same AI, now alliance-wide
established.plain: NATO bought its own variant of Maven for its Allied Command Operations, finalising the deal on 25 March 2025 in roughly six months — one of the fastest acquisitions in NATO's history — and began using it within about 30 days. It is meant to speed up intelligence fusion, targeting and planning across the alliance.

inProgress.label: One mega-deal replacing 75 contracts
inProgress.plain: In July 2025 the Army folded around 75 separate Palantir contracts (15 prime, 60 related) into a single 10-year agreement with a ceiling of up to $10bn — its largest ever with the company. The point is faster buying and volume discounts; the figure is a maximum cap, not a committed spend. Other DoD agencies can also buy Palantir's commercial products through it.

inProgress.label: Demand outgrew the original deal
inProgress.plain: In May 2025 the DoD lifted the Maven Smart System ceiling by $795m to nearly $1.3bn through 2029, citing 'growing demand' from combatant commands using the software for command and control. It signals Maven moving from a pilot toward a routine operational tool.

inProgress.label: Finishing the first 10 units
inProgress.plain: Palantir is still building out the initial order of 10 TITAN systems (five advanced, five basic), aiming to finish by 2026, after which the Army decides on full-rate production — potentially 100-150 units. Further deliveries were expected through 2025-2026.

inProgress.label: Becoming the UK's defence data vendor
inProgress.plain: In late January 2026 the UK Ministry of Defence awarded Palantir a £240.6m, three-year deal for continued data-analytics licensing and support across classifications, interoperable with NATO. Notably it was awarded without a competition, using a defence-and-security exemption — which is part of why it drew scrutiny.

latest.claim: Maven transition onto the Army mega-deal
latest.label: Maven moves to the $10bn vehicle
latest.plain: By April 2026, the DoD was moving Maven Smart System procurement onto the Army Enterprise Agreement as its single contract vehicle, on what officials called an 'aggressive' timeline. In effect, the headline Pentagon AI program and the big consolidated Army deal are being merged into one pipeline.

latest.claim: UK committee urges a 2027 break-clause review
latest.label: Vendor-lock-in scrutiny in the UK
latest.plain: A UK parliamentary committee called Palantir 'the most concerning tech provider' in public-sector digital services and suggested the government consider using a 2027 break clause, citing over-reliance on one vendor and opacity around contract terms. It shows the political risk attached to Palantir's growing UK government role.

suppliers.name: US Department of Defense (DoD CDAO)
suppliers.role: Key customer — Maven Smart System, via the Chief Digital and Artificial Intelligence Office

suppliers.name: US Army
suppliers.role: Key customer — TITAN ground systems and the up-to-$10bn Enterprise Agreement

suppliers.name: NATO (NCIA / Allied Command Operations)
suppliers.role: Customer — Maven Smart System NATO variant

suppliers.name: UK Ministry of Defence
suppliers.role: Customer — £240.6m data-analytics licensing and support deal

suppliers.name: Microsoft
suppliers.role: Partner — delivering Palantir analytics/AI on classified Azure Government networks

unknowns: Public figures are mostly contract ceilings (maximum potential value), not actual obligated or spent amounts, so real spend is unclear. The specific operational deployments, classified use cases and which models run inside AIP are not disclosed, and UK contract terms have been criticised as opaque.
